cuisine: Western / International — Singapore retail commercial bread
food_subgroup: Commercial multigrain bread — wheat flour base with added whole grains (oats, barley, millet, sunflower seeds); packaged sliced loaf

ontology.foodex2_description: Commercial multigrain wheat bread with added whole grains
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercial multigrain bread; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 slices of commercially sliced multigrain bread, weighing approximately 60g, as commonly consumed for breakfast or as sandwich bread in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Multigrain Bread — Gardenia Singapore product label nutritional panel + Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Healthier Choice Symbol nutritional data; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for multigrain bread
nutrient_source.organization: Gardenia Bakeries (Singapore) Pte Ltd; Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Gardenia Singapore product label and HPB Healthier Choice Symbol programme data for commercial Singapore multigrain bread. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 325871, bread multigrain) for plausibility. Gardenia label values used as primary; USDA used for micronutrient gap-filling where label data was not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — multigrain bread; University of Sydney GI Database entry for multigrain bread (range 40–48, representative value 43)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 43 is within the published University of Sydney GI Database range for multigrain bread (40–48). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (43 × 28.0) / 100 = 12.0; input record states 10.8 using 25.1g available carbohydrate per 60g serving (46.7 × 0.60 = 28.0g total carb; subtracting fibre: 28.0 − 2.8 = 25.2g available carb; GL = 43 × 25.2 / 100 = 10.8). Confidence 0.9 per input; retained as referenced assessment.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 525mg per 100g; approximately 315mg per standard 2-slice (60g) serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit multigrain bread to 1–2 slices per meal and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully, as two servings per day (breakfast and lunch) from bread alone can contribute 630mg sodium — approximately 27% of the 2,300mg daily limit — before accounting for spreads, fillings, or other dietary sodium sources.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic index ~43 (low) and glycemic load ~10.8 per 2-slice serving; significantly better than white bread (GI ~73, GL ~17.5) but still a moderate glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Multigrain bread is a preferable carbohydrate choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes compared to white bread due to its lower GI (~43) and higher dietary fibre (4.7g/100g), which moderates postprandial glucose response; however, portion control remains important as each 2-slice serving delivers approximately 25g of available carbohydrate, and rye crispbread (GI ~35) or 100% wholegrain bread remain nutritionally superior alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), zero trans fat, moderate dietary fibre (4.7g/100g), and zero cholesterol; sodium remains a consideration at 525mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Multigrain bread is a heart-friendly bread choice due to its very low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), zero trans fat, and moderate soluble fibre content from oats and barley which may contribute to LDL cholesterol reduction; the primary cardiovascular caution is its relatively high sodium content (525mg/100g), which should be factored into total daily sodium management for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Selenium (~12mcg/100g), B vitamins (B1, B3), and grain diversity providing broader phytochemical range than single-grain bread
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Multigrain bread provides selenium (~7.2mcg per 2-slice serving) supporting antioxidant defence, along with B vitamins from diverse grain inclusions; its low saturated fat content is consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain bread (Singapore retail) refers to commercially produced, packaged sliced bread sold under brands such as Gardenia and Sunshine (F&N) at major Singapore supermarkets including NTUC FairPrice and Cold Storage. It is made from a wheat flour base enriched with multiple whole grains and seeds — typically oats, barley, millet, and sunflower seeds — baked into a soft, slightly chewy loaf with a mild nutty flavour and a tender crumb. Per 100g, it provides approximately 263 kcal, 8.3g protein, 4.2g fat (0.8g saturated), 46.7g carbohydrate, 4.7g dietary fibre, and 525mg sodium. Per standard 2-slice serving (~60g): ~158 kcal, 5g protein, 2.5g fat, 28g carbohydrate, 2.8g fibre, and ~315mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 43 (low GI), yielding a glycemic load of ~10.8 per serving — substantially lower than white bread (GI ~73, GL ~17.5). Many Singapore multigrain breads carry the HPB Healthier Choice Symbol (HCS), indicating they meet defined whole grain and fibre criteria. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: 'multigrain' does not automatically mean 'wholegrain' — consumers should verify that wholemeal flour appears as the first ingredient on the label. Gardenia holds over 50% of Singapore's bread market share. This bread is commonly used for breakfast toast, kaya toast alternatives, and sandwich preparation. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets. Clinically, it is a preferable carbohydrate choice for Type 2 Diabetes management and cardiovascular health compared to white bread, though sodium content warrants monitoring for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: is multigrain bread good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from Gardenia Singapore product label and HPB Healthier Choice Symbol programme data — both approved primary sources for Singapore commercial food products. GI value of 43 is within the published University of Sydney GI Database range for multigrain bread (40–48). Micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plausibility. All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and internally consistent.
notes: 1. ZERO-VALUE JUSTIFICATION: cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per Gardenia product label (plant-based bread product, no animal-derived fat ingredients). vitamin_c_mg is naturally zero as multigrain bread contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients and baking destroys any residual ascorbic acid. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per product label — no fortification with vitamin A in this product. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as this is a plant-based bread product with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per product label — no vitamin D fortification in this product. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per Gardenia product label. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported on the Gardenia Singapore product label and is not available in the HPB database for this specific product; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 325871) provides partial fatty acid data for generic multigrain bread but was not used to fill these fields to avoid mixing source granularity. water_g is set to -1.0 as moisture content is not reported on the Singapore retail product label and was not available in the HPB database for this specific product. 3. MULTIGRAIN vs WHOLEGRAIN CAVEAT: 'multigrain' does not guarantee 'wholegrain' status. Consumers should verify that wholemeal flour is listed as the first ingredient. Gardenia Multigrain Softmeal and Sunshine Multi-grain both carry the HPB Healthier Choice Symbol. 4. SODIUM NOTE: At 525mg/100g (315mg per 2-slice serving), sodium content is relatively high for a bread product and warrants monitoring for hypertensive individuals consuming multiple servings daily. 5. GI GL CALCULATION: GL per serving = (GI 43 × available carbohydrate 25.2g per 60g serving) / 100 = 10.8. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate (28.0g) minus dietary fibre (2.8g) = 25.2g. 6. SOURCE NOTE: Gardenia Singapore website (gardenia.com.sg) and HPB HCS programme are approved primary sources for Singapore commercial packaged food products per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
